past_medical_history: Hypothyroidism, diagnosed 8 years ago, well-controlled on levothyroxine
No history of tuberculosis or known TB exposure
family_history: Uncle (paternal) died of pulmonary tuberculosis in India 10 years ago
Mother alive at age 68, type 2 diabetes mellitus
No family history of autoimmune disease or immunodeficiency

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, 1-2 per month
social_history.occupation: Software architect at a technology company
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two children (ages 8 and 11) in suburban home
social_history.recent_travel: Returned from a 3-week visit to family in rural Maharashtra, India approximately 6 weeks ago

neurological_exam.mental_status: GCS 13 (E3V4M6). Mildly confused, oriented to person and place but incorrectly states the date by 10 days. Speech is fluent but slow. Concentration impaired — unable to perform serial sevens. Registration 3/3, recall 1/3 at five minutes. No aphasia or dysarthria.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal 3 mm, reactive to light bilaterally. Bilateral CN VI palsy — limited lateral gaze bilaterally with esotropia at primary gaze. Left CN VII palsy, lower motor neuron type — flattened left nasolabial fold, weakness of left forehead, incomplete left eye closure. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Visual fields full to confrontation. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Normal muscle tone and bulk throughout. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Mildly hyperreflexic at 3+ bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Not formally tested — patient too confused and unsteady to ambulate safely.
neurological_exam.additional: Mild nuchal rigidity — resistance to passive neck flexion. Kernig sign positive bilaterally. Brudzinski sign equivocal. Photophobia present. No petechiae or purpura on skin examination. Weight loss of approximately 4 kg over the past month per patient's husband. Night sweats reported over past 2-3 weeks.

patient.chief_complaint: Worsening headache and confusion over 2 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Priya Sharma is a 42-year-old right-handed Indian-born woman (US resident for 15 years) who presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of progressively worsening headache, low-grade fevers, malaise, and increasing confusion. She returned from a 3-week visit to her family in rural Maharashtra, India approximately 6 weeks prior to presentation. The headache began insidiously as a dull, generalized ache that she initially attributed to jet lag and stress from returning to work. Over the first week, the headache intensified and she developed low-grade fevers with a maximum temperature of 38.3°C, night sweats, and decreased appetite with unintentional weight loss of approximately 4 kg. She was seen at an urgent care center 5 days ago, where she was diagnosed with sinusitis and prescribed amoxicillin 875 mg twice daily. Her symptoms continued to worsen despite the antibiotic. Three days ago, her husband noted she was increasingly confused, forgetting conversations and asking the same questions repeatedly. Two days ago, she developed new-onset diplopia (seeing double when looking to either side) and worsening photophobia. Yesterday, her husband noticed asymmetry in her face with drooping on the left side. He brought her to the emergency department this morning when she could not recall the current date or her children's school names. She denies cough, hemoptysis, or pulmonary symptoms. She denies rash, joint pains, or visual loss. She has no history of HIV testing but denies risk factors. She had BCG vaccination in childhood in India. Her paternal uncle died of tuberculosis in India 10 years ago, but she reports no known direct exposure to active TB cases.

findings.frequency: 5-7 Hz theta and intermittent 2-3 Hz delta activity
findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic, medium amplitude theta-delta activity diffusely distributed
findings.state: Present during wakefulness, increasing during drowsiness

artifacts.description: Intermittent frontalis and temporalis muscle artifact

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving at 10-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: No epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures captured during this 30-minute recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 30 minutes in a confused, intermittently uncooperative patient.

findings.location: Predominantly at the base of the brain — basal cisterns, interpeduncular fossa, sylvian fissures, and prepontine cistern

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Thick, nodular leptomeningeal enhancement along the basal meninges, extending into the sylvian fissures bilaterally
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Leptomeningeal FLAIR hyperintensity in the basal cisterns
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in the brain parenchyma
signal_characteristics.T2: Mild periventricular edema consistent with early hydrocephalus

findings.mass_effect: Mild ventriculomegaly — lateral ventricles mildly dilated with rounding of the frontal horns, suggesting early communicating hydrocephalus

findings.location: Bilateral basal ganglia (3 lesions in the left caudate/putamen, 2 in the right lentiform nucleus)

signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion within the lesions

findings.mass_effect: None — lesions too small for significant mass effect

additional_observations: Mild communicating hydrocephalus with ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal prominence
No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No cerebritis or abscess formation in the brain parenchyma beyond the small ring-enhancing lesions
Normal vascular flow voids — no vasculitis pattern
mri.impression: Early communicating hydrocephalus with mild ventriculomegaly. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 88 bpm. No conduction abnormalities. No ST-T wave changes. Normal QTc interval.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 12.2 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 72 % (H), Lymphocytes 18 % (L), Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 34.2 % (L), Sodium 128 mEq/L (L), Chloride 94 mEq/L (L), CRP 48 mg/L (H), ESR 62 mm/hr (H), Procalcitonin 0.4 ng/mL (H), Albumin 3.2 g/dL (L), QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus Positive  (H), TSH 4.8 mIU/L (H), Serum osmolality 268 mOsm/kg (L).
abnormal_values_summary: Procalcitonin 0.4 ng/mL (borderline — lower than expected for bacterial meningitis)

special_tests.Gram_stain: No organisms seen on Gram stain
special_tests.AFB_smear: Rare acid-fast bacilli seen on concentrated specimen (Ziehl-Neelsen stain)
special_tests.TB_PCR_GeneXpert: Positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex, rifampin resistance NOT detected
special_tests.Adenosine_deaminase: 18 U/L (elevated; normal <10, suggestive of TB meningitis when >10)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 28 cmH2O. WBC: 220 cells/uL (10% PMN/70% lymph). Protein: 185 mg/dL. Glucose: 22 mg/dL (ratio 0.22 (serum glucose 98 mg/dL)). Gram_stain: No organisms seen on Gram stain. Bacterial_culture: No growth at 48 hours. India_ink: Negative. Cryptococcal_antigen: Negative. AFB_smear: Rare acid-fast bacilli seen on concentrated specimen (Ziehl-Neelsen stain). TB_PCR_GeneXpert: Positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex, rifampin resistance NOT detected. CSF_lactate: 5.8 mmol/L (reference <2.8). Adenosine_deaminase: 18 U/L (elevated; normal <10, suggestive of TB meningitis when >10).

Mycobacterial Culture.value: Fully susceptible: isoniazid (MIC 0.1 mcg/mL), rifampin (MIC 0.5 mcg/mL), ethambutol (MIC 2.5 mcg/mL), pyrazinamide (susceptible), streptomycin (susceptible)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: CSF mycobacterial culture (MGIT liquid medium) Mycobacterium tuberculosis isolated at 21 days  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: CSF mycobacterial culture: M. tuberculosis isolated at 21 days (H)
Drug susceptibility: Fully susceptible to RIPE (H)

signal_characteristics.CT: Densely calcified nodule with no surrounding ground-glass opacity or cavitation
Mildly enlarged with some showing partial calcification

additional_observations: No active pulmonary infiltrates or consolidation
output.impression: Old calcified granuloma in the right upper lobe, likely representing healed prior granulomatous infection (TB or histoplasmosis). No evidence of active pulmonary tuberculosis. The findings suggest prior TB exposure with latent/healed pulmonary disease, with the current CNS disease likely representing hematogenous reactivation from the old focus. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 22 cmH2O. WBC: 85 cells/uL (improved from 220) (3% PMN/85% lymph). Protein: 120 mg/dL. Glucose: 35 mg/dL (ratio 0.36 (serum glucose 97 mg/dL)). AFB_smear: Negative on concentrated specimen. CSF_lactate: 4.2 mmol/L (improved from 5.8).

Ophthalmology Consultation.value: Two choroidal tubercles identified — small, round, yellowish-white lesions with indistinct borders in the posterior pole
One choroidal tubercle identified near the macula. No papilledema. Mild venous congestion.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Visual acuity - Left eye 20/30  (H), Fundoscopic exam - Right eye Two choroidal tubercles identified — small, round, yellowish-white lesions with indistinct borders in the posterior pole  (H), Fundoscopic exam - Left eye One choroidal tubercle identified near the macula. No papilledema. Mild venous congestion.  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Bilateral choroidal tubercles — pathognomonic for disseminated tuberculosis

differential.key_distinguishing: Lymphocytic predominance, subacute 2-week course, very low glucose but low procalcitonin (0.4), and negative bacterial cultures all favor TB meningitis over typical pyogenic bacterial meningitis. Bacterial meningitis is typically hyperacute with neutrophilic predominance and procalcitonin >2.0.

HIV negative, India ink negative, cryptococcal antigen negative. Cryptococcal meningitis can present similarly with basilar meningitis and elevated opening pressure, but is much more common in immunocompromised patients.
Basilar meningitis and cranial neuropathies can occur in sarcoidosis, but positive AFB smear and GeneXpert conclusively confirm TB. ACE level and chest CT findings would differ in sarcoid.
Ring-enhancing lesions are tuberculomas, not tumors. CSF cytology would differentiate. The infectious syndrome with fevers, night sweats, and weight loss is more consistent with TB.

optimal_actions.action: Perform focused history identifying subacute course, travel to TB-endemic region, family TB history, weight loss, and night sweats as key risk factors for tuberculosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: High pretest probability for TB meningitis based on epidemiologic risk factors

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with contrast before lumbar puncture given signs of raised ICP (bilateral CN VI palsy, confusion)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Basilar meningeal enhancement, tuberculomas in basal ganglia, early hydrocephalus

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement, send CSF for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, bacterial culture, AFB smear and culture, TB PCR (GeneXpert), India ink, cryptococcal antigen, and adenosine deaminase
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, very high protein, very low glucose, positive AFB smear, positive GeneXpert for M. tuberculosis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, HIV testing, QuantiFERON-TB Gold, and hepatic panel (baseline before anti-TB therapy)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive QuantiFERON, hyponatremia (SIADH), elevated inflammatory markers, low procalcitonin, HIV negative

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric anti-TB treatment immediately: isoniazid + rifampin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol (RIPE) plus adjunctive dexamethasone — do NOT wait for mycobacterial culture results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric RIPE regimen plus dexamethasone initiated based on clinical and CSF findings

optimal_actions.action: Add adjunctive dexamethasone (0.4 mg/kg/day, tapered over 6-8 weeks) — proven mortality benefit in TB meningitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dexamethasone reduces mortality by approximately 30% in TB meningitis (Thwaites et al., NEJM 2004)

optimal_actions.action: Order chest CT to evaluate for active pulmonary TB and determine need for respiratory isolation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Old calcified granuloma, no active pulmonary TB — but sputum AFB still needed

optimal_actions.action: Monitor for hydrocephalus progression — serial neurological assessments and consider repeat imaging if clinical deterioration
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Assessment for need of CSF diversion (external ventricular drain or ventriculoperitoneal shunt)

optimal_actions.action: Request ophthalmology consultation for dilated fundoscopic exam to look for choroidal tubercles and papilledema
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Choroidal tubercles — pathognomonic for disseminated TB

optimal_actions.action: Perform repeat lumbar puncture at 2 weeks to assess treatment response
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improving CSF parameters but persistent abnormalities — expected in TB meningitis

critical_actions: Obtain CSF with AFB stain and TB PCR (GeneXpert) — do not rely solely on bacterial cultures
Start empiric anti-TB treatment (RIPE + dexamethasone) without waiting for mycobacterial culture
Add adjunctive dexamethasone (proven mortality benefit in TBM)
Assess for hydrocephalus and monitor for need for CSF diversion
Test for HIV (TB meningitis can be the presenting illness)
Assess cranial nerves serially — basilar meningitis causes CN palsies
contraindicated_actions: Discharging after negative bacterial cultures without considering TB
Withholding steroids (dexamethasone reduces mortality in TBM)
Performing LP without imaging in a patient with signs of raised ICP
Prescribing fluoroquinolones empirically (masks TB diagnosis by partially treating it)
key_reasoning_points: Subacute course (2 weeks) with lymphocytic CSF is the hallmark of TB meningitis — bacterial meningitis is typically hyperacute
CSF glucose:serum ratio <0.3 with lymphocytic pleocytosis = strong TB meningitis indicator
Basilar meningitis pattern on MRI with ring-enhancing lesions (tuberculomas) is classic for CNS TB
Low procalcitonin (<0.5) argues against typical bacterial meningitis
Positive QuantiFERON + travel to endemic area + family history of TB = high pretest probability
CN VI palsy bilaterally results from basilar meningitis causing raised ICP
SIADH (Na 128) is common in TBM due to hypothalamic involvement
BCG vaccination does NOT protect against TB meningitis in adults (wanes after childhood)

red_herrings.intended_effect: May falsely reassure that patient is protected against TB
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: BCG protection wanes in adulthood and does not prevent TB meningitis
Procalcitonin is often low in TB meningitis — it is most elevated in bacterial sepsis

red_herrings.intended_effect: May lead clinician away from TB diagnosis
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: TB meningitis occurs in only 1-5% of TB cases and can occur without active pulmonary TB (hematogenous spread from old focus)

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Subacute meningitis initially treated as bacterial but cultures negative. Lymphocytic CSF with very low glucose and basilar enhancement on MRI point to TB meningitis in a patient with epidemiologic risk factors. Low procalcitonin, BCG history, and absence of pulmonary symptoms may mislead.
clinical_pearls: CSF glucose:serum ratio <0.3 with lymphocytic predominance is highly suggestive of TB meningitis
GeneXpert TB PCR on CSF has ~60-80% sensitivity and >95% specificity — a positive result is diagnostic
Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces mortality in TB meningitis (Thwaites et al., NEJM 2004)
Choroidal tubercles on fundoscopy are pathognomonic for disseminated TB
TB meningitis without active pulmonary TB is well-recognized — hematogenous reactivation from latent foci
